bundle_title: AP CSP Create Task Planning + Rubric Checklist Pack

planning_prompts.prompt: Describe your program’s purpose and target user.
List your input(s) and output(s) and how they are produced.
Identify one list (or collection) you will use and why it’s necessary.
Describe the algorithm that uses sequencing, selection, and iteration.
Write a short test plan: 3 tests with expected vs actual results.

items.check: Purpose is clear and specific.
Uses a list/collection and explains why it manages complexity.
Algorithm includes sequencing, selection, and iteration.
Student-developed procedure with parameter(s) is shown.
Includes at least two test cases with expected results.

feedback_templates: Add one sentence explaining how your list makes the program easier to update or expand.
Show where selection (if/else) changes behavior for different inputs.
Include one more test that targets an edge case.

items.check: Written response identifies the program's purpose in one clear sentence.
Describes what the program does (inputs → processing → outputs).
Video shows the program running and producing at least one output.
If no user input is required, that is stated explicitly.
Program uses a list (or other collection type).
The list stores more than one element.
Response explains how the list manages complexity (vs. individual variables).
The list is named in the written response and highlighted in code.
Explicitly explains why using a list simplifies the program.
Compares the list approach to writing many individual variables.
Connects the complexity-management explanation back to the program's purpose.
Procedure is student-developed (not a built-in library call).
Procedure has at least one parameter that affects its behavior.
Procedure name reflects what it does.
Procedure definition is highlighted in the code segment.
Algorithm shows sequencing (steps in order).
Algorithm shows selection (if / else logic).
Algorithm shows iteration (loop).
Sequencing, selection, and iteration all occur inside the student-developed procedure.
A call to the student-developed procedure is highlighted.
The call passes an argument to the parameter.
The argument changes program behavior in an observable way.
Response describes at least two different procedure calls.
Each call's conditions (argument values) are stated.
Each call's result is stated.
The two calls produce different results because of different arguments.
Written response confirms the code is the student's own work.
Any borrowed code, libraries, or ideas are cited.
No use of prohibited generative AI tools during the task.
Video (≤1 min) is uploaded in an accepted format.
Written response PDF is uploaded.
Full program code PDF is uploaded.
Required code segments (list, procedure, call) are highlighted in the code PDF.
